title: OpenStack Control-Plane RabbitMQ Alarms, Connection Churn, and Partition or Queue-Depth Stress
exclusions: **In scope**: Rabbit brokers that carry oslo messaging for on-prem or self-managed OpenStack control planes (Kolla-Ansible, TripleO, Red Hat OSP-style, SUSE OpenCloud, Ubuntu OpenStack Charms) where the Splunk Add-on for RabbitMQ (or REST API Modular Input 1546) can poll `/api/nodes` and `/api/queues`, plus a Universal Forwarder can `monitor://` `/var/log/rabbitmq/rabbit@<host>.log` and the matching `*-sasl.log`, with optional SC4S or rsyslog imfile carrying the rabbitmq-server program tag for journald or syslog overflow. **Out of scope**: UC-19.1.46 Galera or MariaDB wsrep health (data plane SQL quorum and split-brain, not AMQP messaging); UC-19.1.44-style Nova API or libvirt spawn rates and build-failure log metrics (that UC looks at compute-adjacent API paths and ServiceLogger lines, not Rabbit Management JSON or Erlang beam logs); Rabbit used only for Celery, Airflow, IoT MQTT bridges, or RPC fan-out where there is no Oslo namespace at all (no `oslo_messaging_rpc`, no `notifications.info`, no Nova-conductor queue family); public SaaS message services where you cannot run Rabbit Management on the controllers (the JSON API path simply does not exist); managed Amazon MQ for RabbitMQ, Azure Service Bus, or Google Cloud Pub/Sub-style brokers where the file path, alarm semantics, and node identity diverge enough that the SPL would mis-bucket; Ceph, oVirt, Nutanix, and VMware UCs elsewhere in cat-19 that do not ingest `rabbitmq:management:api:json` or `rabbitmq:server` and therefore have no row in this detector. This UC is Rabbit-truth-first; it does not replace HAProxy VIP, Keystone token churn, or TLS certificate rotation analytics, and a green result here does not by itself prove the rest of the OpenStack control plane is healthy.
dataSources: `index=openstack` with a deliberate triple feed like UC-19.1.46 (not Galera) but for the OpenStack messaging plane. (1) `sourcetype=**rabbitmq:management:api:json**` from the Splunk Add-on for RabbitMQ (REST poll of `GET /api/nodes` for `mem_alarm`, `disk_free_alarm`, `running`, `partitions`, and `GET /api/queues` for `messages_ready`, `name` of queues like `oslo_messaging_rpc`, `notifications.info`, and conductor RPC; optional `GET /api/healthchecks/node` and `GET /api/connections` for churn if your add-on or wrapper emits those as JSON lines). (2) `sourcetype=**rabbitmq:server**` via Universal Forwarder `monitor://` of `/var/log/rabbitmq/rabbit@<hostname>.log` (Erlang broker log: memory/disk/FD resource limit alarm language, network partition text, channel/connection churn). (3) `sourcetype=rabbitmq:sasl` for `/var/log/rabbitmq/rabbit@<host>-sasl.log` (OTP/HiPE/supervisor fatals). (4) `sourcetype=syslog` when program=`rabbitmq-server` (SC4S rabbit/rabbitmq vendor filter is an optional front door; rsyslog imfile is a fallback). (5) Optional: `sourcetype=linux:journald` (or your site’s journal sourcetype) for `rabbitmq-server.service` or cgroup OOM kill lines. Do not invent ad-hoc sourcetype names that never appear in your index.
app: **Splunk Add-on for RabbitMQ** (search Splunkbase for the current listing; after install, open `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/<the_app>/default/inputs.conf` and copy the real modular-input stanza names—do not assume a static Splunkbase app number, because listings rotate) plus Universal Forwarder for `monitor://` on `rabbitmq:server` and `rabbitmq:sasl`; optional Splunk Connect for Syslog to normalize `syslog` with program=`rabbitmq-server`; optional Splunkbase REST API Modular Input (1546) if policy prefers a generic HTTPS poller for `/api/*`
spl:
index=openstack (sourcetype="rabbitmq:management:api:json" OR sourcetype="rabbitmq:server" OR sourcetype="rabbitmq:sasl" OR (sourcetype="syslog" (Program="rabbitmq-server" OR program="rabbitmq-server" OR appname="rabbitmq-server"))) earliest=-15m
| eval src_kind=case(sourcetype="rabbitmq:management:api:json", "api", sourcetype="rabbitmq:server", "rlog", sourcetype="rabbitmq:sasl", "sasl", 1=1, "sys")
| eval mema=if(src_kind="api" AND (match(tostring(coalesce(mem_alarm, memAlarm, mem_alarm, "false")), "[Tt]rue") OR tostring(coalesce(mem_alarm, memAlarm)) IN ("1", "1.0")), 1, 0)
| eval diska=if(src_kind="api" AND (match(tostring(coalesce(disk_free_alarm, disk_free_limit_alarm, diskFreeAlarm, "false")), "[Tt]rue") OR tostring(coalesce(disk_free_alarm, disk_free_limit_alarm, diskFreeAlarm)) IN ("1", "1.0")), 1, 0)
| eval part_txt=coalesce(mvindex(partitions,0), tostring(partitions), "")
| eval is_part_api=if(src_kind="api" AND len(part_txt)>2 AND part_txt!="[]" AND part_txt!=" null", 1, 0)
| eval rmq_running=if(src_kind!="api", 1, if(match(tostring(coalesce(running, Running, "true")), "[Ff]alse|^0$"), 0, 1))
| eval qn=coalesce(name, queue, queue_name, queueName, qname, "")
| eval mready=tonumber(coalesce(messages_ready, messagesReady, message_ready, "0"))
| eval crit_q=if(src_kind="api" AND match(qn, "(?i)oslo_messaging_rpc|notifications\\.info|conductor$") AND mready>2000, 1, 0)
| eval n_part_l=if(src_kind!="api" AND match(_raw, "(?i)(network partition|mnesia has suspended|mnesia is overloaded|mnesia.*partition)"), 1, 0)
| eval memdisk_l=if(src_kind!="api" AND match(_raw, "(?i)(resource limit alarm|low on disk|file descriptor)"), 1, 0)
| eval oom_l=if(src_kind!="api" AND match(_raw, "(?i)(out of memory|oom-kill|Killed process|oom kill)"), 1, 0)
| eval churn_l=if(src_kind!="api" AND match(_raw, "(?i)(closing (AMQP|connection)|connection (closed|closed abnormally)|Channel exception|handshake timeout)"), 1, 0)
| eval os_peer=if(match(_raw, "(?i)nova-compute|neutron-server|cinder-volume|heat-engine|glance-api|keystone"), 1, 0)
| stats max(_time) as _time, max(mema) as mem_alarm_f, max(diska) as disk_alarm_f, min(rmq_running) as run_min, max(is_part_api) as part_api, sum(n_part_l) as n_part_l, sum(memdisk_l) as memdisk_n, sum(oom_l) as oom_n, sum(churn_l) as churn_n, sum(os_peer) as os_peer_n, max(crit_q) as crit_q, max(mready) as ready_max, values(qn) as sample_queues by host
| eval severity_bucket=case(mem_alarm_f=1 OR disk_alarm_f=1 OR run_min=0 OR part_api=1 OR oom_n>0, "critical", memdisk_n>=3 OR n_part_l>=1 OR crit_q=1 OR (churn_n>=25 AND os_peer_n>10) OR (churn_n>=40), "warning", memdisk_n>0 OR n_part_l>0 OR churn_n>=6, "watch", 1=1, "ok")
| eval sev_order=case(severity_bucket="critical", 1, severity_bucket="warning", 2, severity_bucket="watch", 3, 1=1, 4)
| sort 0 sev_order host
| table _time, host, mem_alarm_f, disk_alarm_f, run_min, part_api, n_part_l, memdisk_n, oom_n, churn_n, os_peer_n, crit_q, ready_max, sample_queues, severity_bucket, sev_order
description: This use case is the **OpenStack control-plane RabbitMQ** detector. It fuses a JSON poll of the RabbitMQ Management HTTP API ( `/api/nodes`, optional `/api/queues`, and optional health/connection material when your collector emits it into `rabbitmq:management:api:json` ) with file and syslog streams (`rabbitmq:server`, `rabbitmq:sasl`, `syslog`) so you see mem_alarm / disk / file descriptor style pressure, Mnesia/pause_minority/partition language, HiPE/OTP or cgroup OOM adjacent lines, and connection/channel churn in the same roll-up. It buckets hosts into a four-tier `**severity_bucket**`, clusters context by `host` (Rabbit node or controller host, depending on how you set UF host), and explicitly does not target OpenStack SQL Galera split-brain (UC-19.1.46), Nova build/spawn/volume-attach log rates (UC-19.1.44 is API/compute-telemetry, not the broker’s AMQP state), or Celery/IoT Rabbit that is not your private cloud control bus
value: When the **Rabbit cluster** behind Nova, Neutron, Cinder, Heat, and Keystone oslo.rpc hits resource alarms, network partition handling, or queue depth runaway, operator-facing symptoms look like a vague “OpenStack is broken” wave—API timeouts, long RPC calls, stuck tasks, and orchestration that never completes—while the true fault is a single messaging tier starved of RAM, FDs, or disk, or in a split mnesia view. Paging the messaging owners early with SPL-visible evidence separates messaging from DB and compute and reduces MTTR; executives get a clear story that a “small” broker problem can still idle much of a region if RPC and fanout notifications can’t drain
implementation: Index OpenStack **controller**-class data into `index=openstack` with the four canonical **sourcetypes** in `dataSources`. Install the add-on, enable its Management API poller, deploy UF on each Rabbit **node** for both log files, and optionally add SC4S for **syslog** or **linux:journald** for OOM. Save the `spl` on a 5m cadence, alert on `severity_bucket` in `critical` or `warning` with throttling on `host`, and pair with a maintenance lookup for **Pacemaker**/Ansible work windows
visualization: Row A — Single value of worst `sev_order` in the last 15m (by site: optionally split by a `dc` or `az` field if you add it in props). Row B — **Timechart** of `churn_n` and `memdisk_n` per 5m by `host` for burst visibility. Row C — Timechart of `ready_max` for queue names that match the OpenStack RPC and notify patterns in the `spl` field. Row D — **Table** of `host`, `mem_alarm_f`, `disk_alarm_f`, `part_api`, `n_part_l`, `oom_n`, `churn_n`, `os_peer_n`, `crit_q`, `severity_bucket`, with drilldown to `rabbitmq:server` raw in the same minute. Optional heatmap: count of `severity_bucket` by `host` for fleet posture.

references.title: RabbitMQ — Management HTTP API (nodes, queues, health)
Splunk REST API Modular Input (HTTPS polling; Splunkbase 1546)

knownFalsePositives: Planned Kolla or TripleO rolling Rabbit container restarts with a documented change where Mnesia log strings appear but the cluster never loses quorum. Disaster-rehearsal or chaos drills that intentionally pause consumers and spike churn under change control, not a production defect. Federation, shovel, or mirroring load tests in staging that open and close many connections and raise log volume without user-visible outage. A short mem_alarm or disk_alarm during a large Heat stack create or large orchestration batch that clears when the work completes. Rabbit base image or Erlang point upgrades on a canary with scary SASL lines on one node only while others stay healthy. Synthetic pollers in QA that multiply JSON events per minute without hitting production vhosts. Training runs where an engineer runs stop_app and join in lab on a non-quorum node and logs reflect expected mnesia language. Transient mnesia or partition strings while a node rejoins after a vendor drive RMA, before rabbitmqctl shows healthy. Connection or channel limit tests in staging that are intentionally throttled. Defragmentation or queue rebalance under vendor runbook with elevated log volume for a bounded window. A bad props regex in lab that flags a one-line spurious string from an unrelated process—fix routing before you trust the alert. Blue-green internal VIP or HAProxy leg switch rehearsal that elevates connection churn for minutes under a CAB ticket.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• OpenStack control-plane hosts running Rabbit (typically three to five members) with the management plugin enabled, TLS or plain :15672 per your standard, and a read-only `monitoring` user for the HTTP API. Pacemaker, Docker/Podman, or systemd names differ by vendor; this UC is agnostic to packaging as long as the file paths in Step 1 exist or are mapped with an equivalent `monitor://`.
• A Splunk index (this document uses `index=openstack`) that Universal Forwarder, HEC, or a heavy forwarder may write, plus a role with `srchIndexesAllowed` including that index and no broader grant than required.
• Network paths from the collection tier to 15672 (or 443 behind HAProxy) for REST polling, and 9997/splunktcp or HEC to the indexers; nothing in this UC needs AMQP 5672 open to Splunk.
• NTP or chrony on all controller-class hosts: correlation to Nova API logs and this broker telemetry depends on wall-clock agreement within a few seconds.
• A CMDB or inventory tag that names which hosts are Rabbit members so alert routing lands on the platform messaging owner, not the compute-only queue.

**Step 1 — Configure RabbitMQ data collection**

Install the **Splunk Add-on for RabbitMQ** from Splunkbase (use the current listing; copy modular-input stanza names from `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/<app>/default/inputs.conf` into `local/inputs.conf` like UC-19.1.26 instructs for Nutanix, rather than retyping a stanza from a blog screenshot). The add-on (or a wrapper you build) should poll at minimum `GET /api/nodes` for fields such as `mem_alarm`, `disk_free_alarm`, `running`, and `partitions`, and for queue backpressure, `GET /api/queues` covering vhosts that carry Oslo traffic, often including queues whose names include `oslo_messaging_rpc`, `notifications.info`, and conductor class names. Point output at `sourcetype="rabbitmq:management:api:json"`; if a TA version renames the sourcetype, clone this saved search and substitute your on-disk sourcetype after you run `| metadata type=sourcetypes index=openstack` and confirm one canonical string. A generic alternative supported in many shops is the REST API modular input (Splunkbase 1546) with identical HTTPS paths and a forced sourcetype so the `spl` field in this use case does not change.

File-based collection on every Rabbit node uses Universal Forwarder `monitor://` and stable sourcetypes the SPL already unions:

```ini
[monitor:///var/log/rabbitmq/*.log]
index = openstack
sourcetype = rabbitmq:server
disabled = 0
crcSalt = <SOURCE>
```

On builds that split the SASL file away from the main glob, add a second stanza for the `*-sasl.log` path with `sourcetype=rabbitmq:sasl` so OTP supervisor and HiPE issues do not get merged into a props rule meant for the main beam log.

`props.conf` should merge multi-line Erlang diagnostics: keep `SHOULD_LINEMERGE` true for both `rabbitmq:server` and `rabbitmq:sasl`, tune `LINE_BREAKER` to the timestamp pattern printed on your build (Debian, RHEL, and containerized paths differ; sample five `_raw` events in Search before you lock `TIME_PREFIX`). Truncate high but not zero: long partition traces must survive one screen for triage. For props that extract `program` for syslog, ensure `TRANSFORMS` for SC4S metadata does not clobber the host: set `host` from the original IP or DNS name the forwarder used so `stats` by `host` lines up with your API poll host field.

If rsyslog or SC4S already ingests the rabbitmq-server program name, map `sourcetype=syslog` with program=`rabbitmq-server` into the same `openstack` index. Confirm host fields match the physical controller identity you expect in a `stats by host` roll-up, because this UC keys on `host` at the end of the SPL.

Optional journald: when systemd journal collection is in policy, add `sourcetype=linux:journald` to a child search that matches the rabbitmq service unit and OOM killer lines, and merge only after you validate in staging. If you do merge journald into a wider sourcetype, add a `src_kind` eval in a clone of the saved search so you can tell cgroup OOM from arbitrary kernel noise. Document for auditors which path is in scope: file-first is usually enough for the nine failure modes in Step 5; journald is a belt-and-suspenders signal for the OOM case.

Licensing: poll frequency drives event volume. If the modular input defaults to 60s and your estate is large, consider 120s for non-Sev-1 test clusters but keep 60s for production members so `mem_alarm` cannot hide between ticks during a fast ramp. Keep one JSON line per node per tick to avoid log multiplication.

**Step 2 — Create the search and alert**

The `spl` field in this JSON is the report body. Paste it in Settings → Searches, reports, and alerts → New Report → use `Open in Search`, pick Last 15 minutes, and confirm the table shows one row per broker host in steady state, with `severity_bucket` mostly `ok` during a calm window. Save as a report, then Save as Alert.

```spl
index=openstack (sourcetype="rabbitmq:management:api:json" OR sourcetype="rabbitmq:server" OR sourcetype="rabbitmq:sasl" OR (sourcetype="syslog" (Program="rabbitmq-server" OR program="rabbitmq-server" OR appname="rabbitmq-server"))) earliest=-15m
| eval src_kind=case(sourcetype="rabbitmq:management:api:json", "api", sourcetype="rabbitmq:server", "rlog", sourcetype="rabbitmq:sasl", "sasl", 1=1, "sys")
| eval mema=if(src_kind="api" AND (match(tostring(coalesce(mem_alarm, memAlarm, mem_alarm, "false")), "[Tt]rue") OR tostring(coalesce(mem_alarm, memAlarm)) IN ("1", "1.0")), 1, 0)
| eval diska=if(src_kind="api" AND (match(tostring(coalesce(disk_free_alarm, disk_free_limit_alarm, diskFreeAlarm, "false")), "[Tt]rue") OR tostring(coalesce(disk_free_alarm, disk_free_limit_alarm, diskFreeAlarm)) IN ("1", "1.0")), 1, 0)
| eval part_txt=coalesce(mvindex(partitions,0), tostring(partitions), "")
| eval is_part_api=if(src_kind="api" AND len(part_txt)>2 AND part_txt!="[]" AND part_txt!=" null", 1, 0)
| eval rmq_running=if(src_kind!="api", 1, if(match(tostring(coalesce(running, Running, "true")), "[Ff]alse|^0$"), 0, 1))
| eval qn=coalesce(name, queue, queue_name, queueName, qname, "")
| eval mready=tonumber(coalesce(messages_ready, messagesReady, message_ready, "0"))
| eval crit_q=if(src_kind="api" AND match(qn, "(?i)oslo_messaging_rpc|notifications\\.info|conductor$") AND mready>2000, 1, 0)
| eval n_part_l=if(src_kind!="api" AND match(_raw, "(?i)(network partition|mnesia has suspended|mnesia is overloaded|mnesia.*partition)"), 1, 0)
| eval memdisk_l=if(src_kind!="api" AND match(_raw, "(?i)(resource limit alarm|low on disk|file descriptor)"), 1, 0)
| eval oom_l=if(src_kind!="api" AND match(_raw, "(?i)(out of memory|oom-kill|Killed process|oom kill)"), 1, 0)
| eval churn_l=if(src_kind!="api" AND match(_raw, "(?i)(closing (AMQP|connection)|connection (closed|closed abnormally)|Channel exception|handshake timeout)"), 1, 0)
| eval os_peer=if(match(_raw, "(?i)nova-compute|neutron-server|cinder-volume|heat-engine|glance-api|keystone"), 1, 0)
| stats max(_time) as _time, max(mema) as mem_alarm_f, max(diska) as disk_alarm_f, min(rmq_running) as run_min, max(is_part_api) as part_api, sum(n_part_l) as n_part_l, sum(memdisk_l) as memdisk_n, sum(oom_l) as oom_n, sum(churn_l) as churn_n, sum(os_peer) as os_peer_n, max(crit_q) as crit_q, max(mready) as ready_max, values(qn) as sample_queues by host
| eval severity_bucket=case(mem_alarm_f=1 OR disk_alarm_f=1 OR run_min=0 OR part_api=1 OR oom_n>0, "critical", memdisk_n>=3 OR n_part_l>=1 OR crit_q=1 OR (churn_n>=25 AND os_peer_n>10) OR (churn_n>=40), "warning", memdisk_n>0 OR n_part_l>0 OR churn_n>=6, "watch", 1=1, "ok")
| eval sev_order=case(severity_bucket="critical", 1, severity_bucket="warning", 2, severity_bucket="watch", 3, 1=1, 4)
| sort 0 sev_order host
| table _time, host, mem_alarm_f, disk_alarm_f, run_min, part_api, n_part_l, memdisk_n, oom_n, churn_n, os_peer_n, crit_q, ready_max, sample_queues, severity_bucket, sev_order
```

The SPL above is the exact copy from the `spl` field; keep them in sync if you edit either. Schedule on cron `*/5 * * * *` with a matching dispatch window (`earliest` -15m in the saved search) so poll cadence and log tails align. Trigger when `severity_bucket` is `critical` or `warning`. Suppress on `host` for fifteen minutes. Send `watch` to chat only in early deployments; do not page it until the team trusts noise levels. If you route to a ticketing integration, pass `host`, `severity_bucket`, and the numeric columns in the default JSON template so a bridge lead can run ad-hoc searches without retyping the SPL. For PagerDuty, set different urgency for `warning` vs `critical` and include a deep link to the report.

**Step 3 — Validate (sibling scope)**

Rerun the mental model before trusting pages: this UC is the messaging bus, not the database quorum in UC-19.1.46 (Galera split-brain), and not the Nova build or spawn log rate in UC-19.1.44 (API-side failure counts). It also excludes non-OpenStack Rabbit estates such as Celery worker farms or IoT bridges where Oslo RPC never appears.

(1) API field presence: `index=openstack sourcetype=rabbitmq:management:api:json | head 1 | fieldsummary` should list `name`, alarm booleans, and `running` as extracted fields, possibly camel-cased; the `spl` use case `coalesce()` covers common variants. (2) Host coverage: the same index over thirty minutes should show one representative event per poller per Rabbit member; a missing `host` means a deployment gap, not health. (3) `rabbitmq-diagnostics` on a canary node should align wall-clock with Splunk `_time` for a synthetic alarm string you can trigger in a lab (memory watermark test). (4) For queue growth, a lab publish burst into `oslo_messaging_rpc` with consumers stopped briefly should raise `ready_max` without crossing production thresholds, proving the `crit_q` path is not dead. (5) Churn: tail `/var/log/rabbitmq` during normal OpenStack work and count connection lines; the SPL sum should be low single digits per minute per host, not hundreds. (6) If `partitions` in JSON is non-empty, run `rabbitmqctl` `cluster_status` on the same host; Splunk is an early flag, the CLI is still authoritative for mnesia state before any `stop_app` sequence. (7) After any change to `props` that extracts fields, spot-check with `| head 100` for multiline events that still break at timestamps. (8) If two UEs ship the same file due to a mis-routed `deploymentclient`, you will see duplicate `host` in `dedup` output; `crcSalt` as written prevents the classic duplicate, but the dedup symptom is a clue. (9) Run the saved search in `fast` and `verbose` mode during validation; the SPL is not `tstats`-bound because CIM is intentionally absent—accept scan cost in trade for first-day correctness, then add summary indexing later in a new UC if the estate demands it.

SPL sibling differentiation: Step 3 above names UC-19.1.46 and UC-19.1.44 explicitly. If your results look like Nova or SQL symptoms, do not “fix” the messaging SPL—pivot to those UCs, then re-check Rabbit after the DB and compute story is clear.

**Step 4 — Tune for your environment**

The defaults (`mready>2000`, churn sums, and watch thresholds) are first-pass. Large telemetry deployments may legitimately spike `notifications.info` for minutes; create a `macros.conf` override for `os_rmq_ready_floor` and `churn_n` per region. Kolla and TripleO may terminate TLS on an internal VIP; HA health checks can add log noise—route HAProxy to its own sourcetype before you filter the Rabbit file. Maintenance windows: a CSV keyed by `host,epoch_start,epoch_end,change_id,owner` can downshift `watch` to an informational route while still allowing `critical` when `mem_alarm` is true in the API. Multi-site stretched clusters, if you still run one logical Rabbit, widen the `earliest` in the saved search to -30m to remove WAN poll jitter. Dashboard layout: a single value for worst `sev_order`, a timechart of `churn_n` and `memdisk_n` by `host`, a timechart of `ready_max` for queue name regex families, and a table sorted by `severity_bucket` and `churn_n`, with a drill to `rabbitmq:server` in the same minute as the table row. Add a text panel that links to the Kolla or TripleO runbook for Rabbit upgrades so on-call can jump from signal to human procedure without a wiki hunt.

**Step 5 — Troubleshooting (nine common failure modes)**

(1) Management API 401 or TLS handshake failures: the modular input is pointed at the wrong vhost, wrong management port, or a cert chain your forwarder does not trust—fix the input stanza, not the SPL. (2) `rabbitmq:server` is empty but the file exists: wrong index or forwarder is not the Linux user that can read `/var/log/rabbitmq`—`chmod` and supplemental groups, or SELinux `audit2allow`. (3) API says healthy while logs show partition: trust logs first, then the CLI; short poll windows can miss a flap—extend `earliest` in an ad-hoc search. (4) `mem_alarm` in JSON true but `free` on the OS is high: the Erlang VM has its own high water marks; `rabbitmq-diagnostics` memory is the right next command. (5) `fd` or connection pressure: inspect `GET /api/connections` in a subsearch to find stale service clients after a control-plane hot loop. (6) `notifications.info` depth without RPC depth: a telemetry or Ceilometer storm; tune queue allowlists, not the whole UC off. (7) SASL shows code_server or HiPE crash while the main log is quiet: read the SASL file; it often has the one-line root cause. (8) OOM in journald only: add optional journald collection from Step 1. (9) All Rabbit signals green but OpenStack 500s persist: this UC is messaging-only; keep Keystone, HAProxy, and Galera UCs in parallel; concurrent failures are common in brownouts.

Coalesce and upgrades follow-up: the public `coalesce()` chain in the `spl` field is what keeps you working when a TA renames a management field from snake_case to camelCase between maintenance releases. When you promote a new Rabbit major (for example 3.13 to 4.x) under Kolla, re-run the fieldsummary checks in Step 3 because flag names around quorum queues and stream fanout can change operator-facing strings even if the SPL still parses. Capacity: each window rolls up to `host` only; if a single `host` field is used for more than one container, add a `rmq_node` field in `transforms.conf` and extend the `by` clause, or you will under-count members. For SC4S users, keep program-specific transforms; otherwise lost program metadata will fall only into the `sys` branch, which is still useful for raw keywords but not for `Program` filters. For pause_minority or pause_if_all_down_recovery, logs often lead API booleans—keep file and API both enabled. For security, never put clear-text passwords in a saved search: keep them in the modular input or a vault wrapper, and do not grant write to the read-only user Splunk uses. For blue-green upgrades, a temporary `churn_n` spike may be change-bound: suppress on change id. After `rabbitmqctl reset` in a lab, SASL can scream—point production alerts at production indexes only.

Indexer side: if you need to mask PII in raw connection names, use a `SEDCMD` in `props.conf` that strips userinfo from `client_properties` text while leaving timing fields intact; re-validate Step 3 counts after any SEDCMD because over-matching can zero the `churn_n` signal. Optional stream filter: a `search` time bound on business hours is a cultural choice, not a product requirement; only apply once you have seen night-batch churn in `notifications.info` and accepted the risk of missing a 03:00 incident.

Closing operational note: keep the triple-stream pattern from UC-19.1.46 in mind—poll, file, syslog—but swap Galera for Rabbit Management and Erlang. That structural mirror is what makes a greenfield private cloud SRE team productive in a single day of pairing with this document: you already know the deployment-server story for inputs; only the file paths and HTTP endpoints change.
grandmaExplanation: We watch the shared sorting hall every important OpenStack service uses to pass work between teams. When that hall is out of room, jams the doors, or the staff disagree on the official pile, people cannot get simple requests done, even when their own computers look fine. We raise the hand early so the right people fix the hall before a whole private cloud looks broken.
hardware: OpenStack controller-class guests (or bare metal 1U/2U Dell/HPE/Supermicro) sized for the **Erlang BEAM** that backs Rabbit 3.x/4.x: typically 8–16 vCPU, 32–64 GiB RAM per node (your Kolla/TripleO flavor list may be smaller in lab), 200+ GiB SSD for log and Mnesia state, 10/25 Gbps to the API and replication VLANs, three to five Rabbit peers in odd-N quorum-friendly layouts. OVS and Ceph are not the subject here, but the control network that carries :5672 and :15672 must be stable; NTP is mandatory so Rabbit log and API poll line up with OpenStack service logs when you join for triage
